title: Valve Type Selection
summary: Double-seated (balanced) valves are used where fluid pressure is too high for single-seated valves to close or modulate properly, but not for tight shutoff.
evidence_quote: A double-seated or balanced valve (Figure 9B) is designed so that the media pressure acting against the valve disk is essentially balanced, reducing the actuator force required. It is widely used where fluid pressure is too high to allow a single-seated valve to close or to modulate properly. It is not usually used where tight shut-off is required.

title: Butterfly Valve Application
summary: Two butterfly valves can be configured (one normally open, one normally closed) to function like a three-way valve for mixing or diverting.
evidence_quote: Two butterfly valves can be used together to act like a three-way valve for mixing or diverting. In this arrangement, one valve is set up as normally open and the other is normally closed.

title: Valve Flow Characteristics
summary: Three common valve flow characteristics are defined: quick opening, linear, and equal percentage.
evidence_quote: Three common characteristics are shown in Figure 10 and are defined as follows: Quick opening. Maximum flow is approached rapidly as the device begins to open. Linear. Opening and flow are related in direct proportion. Equal percentage. Each equal increment of opening increases flow by an equal percentage over the previous value.

title: Valve Authority for Control
summary: For adequate control, the valve's design pressure drop should be a reasonably large percentage of total system pressure drop (adequate valve authority).
evidence_quote: To allow for adequate control by the valve, design pressure drop should be a reasonably large percentage of total system pressure drop at the valve (i.e., adequate valve authority) and the system should be designed and controlled so that pressure drop remains relatively constant.

title: Steam Valve Pressure Drop Limit
summary: Maximum pressure drop for steam valves is a function of operating pressure and cannot be exceeded.
evidence_quote: Maximum pressure drop for steam valves is a function of operating pressure and cannot be exceeded.

title: Controller Tuning: Ziegler-Nichols (Closed-Loop) Method
summary: Formulas for calculating proportional band (PB), integral time (Ti), and derivative time (Td) based on ultimate gain (PBu) and ultimate period (Tu) for P, PI, and PID controllers.
evidence_quote: Proportional only: PB = 1.8(PBu) percent. Proportional plus integral (PI): PB = 2.22(PBu) percent, Ti = 0.83 Tu minute per repeat. Proportional plus integral plus derivative (PID): PB = 1.67(PBu) percent, Ti = 0.50 Tu minute per repeat, Td = 0.125 Tu minute.

title: Controller Tuning: First-Order-plus-Dead-Time (Open-Loop) Method
summary: Formulas for calculating proportional band (PB), integral time (Ti), and derivative time (Td) based on process gain, dead time (TD), and time constant (TC) for P, PI, and PID controllers.
evidence_quote: Proportional only: PB = Gain/(TC/TD). Proportional plus integral (PI): PB = 0.9(Gain)/(TC/TD), Ti = 3.33(TD). Proportional-integral-derivative (PID): PB = 1.2(Gain)/(TC/TD), Ti = 2(TD), Td = 0.5(TD).

title: Digital Controller Sampling Interval
summary: A controller sampling interval of about one-tenth of the controlled-process time constant usually provides adequate control.
evidence_quote: A controller sampling interval of about one-tenth of the controlled-process time constant usually provides adequate control.

title: Building Pressure for Moisture Control
summary: In cooling climates, building pressure should be positive to keep out humid outside air. In heating climates, pressure should be neither strongly negative nor strongly positive.
evidence_quote: In cooling climates, pressure should be positive to keep out humid outside air. In heating climates, pressure should be neither strongly negative, which could risk drawing soil gas or combustion products indoors and affect occupant comfort, nor strongly positive, which could risk driving moisture into building envelope cavities.
